qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: to.e.vah (H8441) and bdelugma (G0946) name a moral-evaluative category applied relationally between persons and God, which has an inner-being dimension (conscience, moral orientation) but both terms are primarily used to designate external acts, objects, and practices rather than internal states.
qualification_assessment.qualification_flag: The word names a relational moral category (what God or persons find repugnant) rather than an internal state residing in the person. However, verses such as Pro 26:25 ('seven abominations in his heart') and Luke 16:15 ('what is exalted among men is an abomination before God') show inner-being referents are present. The category label 'Moral/Conscience' in the source file supports provisional inner-being relevance.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Detestable, abominable. Describes persons or things that are disgusting or morally repugnant.
An abomination or detestable thing; used both of the 'abomination of desolation' (cultic-eschatological) and of idolatry and moral pollution (ethical-relational).

term_inventory.note: Source file marks this term 'Exclude word / This word only' — no term block present.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: A disgusting thing, abomination, abominable; used in both ritual sense (unclean food, idols, mixed marriages) and ethical sense (wickedness).
Detestable thing or idol, abominable thing; used predominantly of foreign idols and cultic objects.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal imperfective first singular either gender
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To denounce, express indignation, be indignant; in Qal: to have indignation, be angrily indignant, be defiant, be abhorrent, denounce or curse in speech. In Niphal: to show indignation or anger.
Detestable thing or idol, unclean thing, abomination, detestation; used primarily of ritually unclean animals and foods.
Horrid thing, horrible thing; used of an abominable idol image.
Foul thing, refuse; used exclusively of unclean or ritually tainted sacrificial flesh.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: Verb Present Middle or Passive Deponent Participle Vocative Singular Masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: In middle voice: to abhor, detest, loathe. In passive voice: to be vile, abhorrent, abominable. The LSJ notes an earlier somatic root in feeling physical loathing for food.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb hiphil consecutive imperfective third singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To abhor, be abominable, do abominably. Niphal: to be abhorred or detested (ritual and ethical). Piel: to loathe, abhor, regard as abomination, or cause to be an abomination. Hiphil: to make abominable, do abominably (ritual and ethical). Note: source file also references ta.av (H8374) 'to loathe' as an alternate form.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb piel imperfective second plural masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: In Piel: to detest, make abominable, count filthy, make detestable; used primarily in levitical purity contexts and one psalm verse showing God's non-abhorrence of the afflicted.

To have a bad smell, stink, smell bad; Qal: literal stench. Niphal: to become or make oneself odious. Hiphil: to stink, emit stench, cause to stink, used figuratively of wickedness. Hithpael: to make oneself odious. TWOT notes an extended meaning 'to abhor'.
Unlawful, criminal, wicked, detestable; derived from themis (divine law or custom). The term carries a sense of violation of divinely established order rather than disgust.

bdelugma.reason: Cultic-eschatological referent (abomination of desolation); no human inner-being subject.
Parallel to Mat 24:15; cultic-eschatological referent with no inner-being subject.
Apocalyptic-symbolic referent (Babylon/harlot); abomination used of systemic moral corruption rather than an individual inner state.

za.am.reason: Narrative context; request to denounce/curse Israel.
Geopolitical-military register; indignation drives political action rather than expressing inner moral orientation.
The verse concerns dishonest measures; the link to za.am is unclear from the translation — possible source data issue.
Divine-temporal register; God's indignation over cities rather than individual inner-being state.

she.qets.reason: Ritual purity; refers to unclean things, not inner states.
Cultic food practice of a specific group; external act.

miph.le.tset.reason: Refers to a physical idol image made for Asherah.
Parallel to 1Ki 15:13; same physical idol referent.

pig.gul.reason: Ritual regulation concerning tainted sacrificial flesh; external.
Repetition of sacrificial taint regulation; external.
Describes illicit cultic food practices; external act.
Ezekiel's protestation against eating tainted meat; external food category.

ta.av.reason: Describes royal idolatrous behaviour; external act.
Applied to a dead king cast out; social-post-mortem register with no inner-being subject.
Servant figure described as abhorred by the nation; corporate-relational register rather than individual inner state.
Refers to external sexual act offered to passers-by.
Social shame and comparative indictment; external acts.

shiq.qets.reason: The instruction 'do not make yourselves detestable' implies an inner consequence of ritual violation — the boundary between external act and inner state is unclear.
Same as Lev 11:43; the reflexive 'make yourselves detestable' suggests an inner-being dimension.

ba.ash.reason: Social-relational register; Jacob's household made odious to Canaanites — no individual inner-being state.
Social-relational register; Israelites made a stench to Pharaoh.
Literal olfactory stench of the Nile; no inner-being referent.
Literal stench of dead frogs; external.
Literal stench of rotting manna; external.
Israel described as a stench to the Philistines; military-social register.
David made himself a stench to Israel; social-political register.
Ammonites a stench to David; social-political register.
Ahithophel's political advice uses stench as metaphor for relational rupture.
Wisdom metaphor using literal stench to illustrate how folly corrupts reputation; indirect inner-being dimension.
Literal stench of dead fish in a dried sea; no inner-being referent.

athemitos.reason: Refers to past lifestyle practices; the term describes external acts rather than an inner state, though Acts 10:28 shows the same term with an inner-conviction dimension.

cross_registry_links.note: ta.av appears as a related word under to.e.vah and as a distinct term in the STEP list; the source file notes a possible alternate form H8374 'to loathe' alongside H8581 'to abhor'.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: za.am (noun H2195) related to za.am (verb H2194)
cross_registry_links.note: The noun form za.am (indignation) is listed as a related word under the verb za.am; both may cross-register with a separate 'indignation' word if present in the 181.
miph.le.tset shares its root with tiph.le.tset (terror) and pa.lats (to shudder), which may correspond to separate entries in the 181.
be.osh (stench) is a nominal derivate of ba.ash and may be a separate entry in the 181.

data_quality_flags.description: makrumma (μάκρυμμα) appears in the STEP related word list with the gloss 'abomination' but has no term block anywhere in the source file; Strong's number, grammatical form, meaning, and verses are entirely absent.

bdelugmos (βδελυγμός) appears in the STEP related word list annotated 'Exclude word / This word only'; no term block is present and the analyst's instruction to exclude is noted but the rationale is not given.

The word analysis block for to.e.vah shows a parsing artifact ('for (ki - כִּי)') preceding the term heading, suggesting the anchor occurrence included a preceding conjunction that STEP parsed as part of the token; this does not affect the term data but is a format irregularity.

The source file records two lexical entries under the same transliteration ta.av: H8581 ('to abhor') which has a full term block, and H8374 ('to loathe') which is mentioned only in the meaning section of H8581 as a cross-reference; H8374 has no independent term block or verse list.

The Mic 6:10 verse ('Can I forget any longer the treasures of wickedness...the scant measure that is accursed?') does not visibly contain the term za.am in the English translation; the connection between this verse and za.am is unclear from the source data alone.

The word analysis block for pig.gul shows parsing tokens ('him (vo - וֹ)', 'to/for (l - /ל)') preceding the term heading, similar to the to.e.vah artifact; this is a STEP anchor-token display issue and does not corrupt the term data.
The word analysis block for shiq.qets shows parsing tokens ('their (hem - הֶם)') preceding the term heading; same STEP anchor-token display issue.
The word analysis block for she.qets shows multiple parsing tokens ('in/on/with (b - /ב)', 'all (kol - כֹּל)', 'or (o - אוֹ)') preceding the term heading; same STEP anchor-token display issue.

Only 2 verses are provided for miph.le.tset despite an occurrence count of 4; the meaning section gives only one bare phrase ('horrid thing, horrible thing') with no sub-definitions or stem information.
The meaning section for pig.gul has no Strong's-style numbered sub-definitions beyond the statement that it is used exclusively of unclean sacrificial flesh; the related words cluster is empty.
